label: Whitfield · 68F · new AFib + complex polypharmacy
subtitle: Outpatient cardiology · presenting with palpitations

demographics.address: 88 Spring Garden Road, Halifax, NS B3J 1A5

presenting_complaint: Two-day history of irregular palpitations, mild dyspnea on exertion, and one episode of near-syncope yesterday. No chest pain. No focal neurological symptoms.

past_medical: Prior transient ischemic attack (2022, no residual deficit)
history.social: Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol. Lives alone. Self-medication management.
history.family: Father had stroke at 72. Mother type 2 diabetes.

examination: Alert and oriented. Irregularly irregular pulse. No JVD. Lungs clear bilaterally. No carotid bruits. Mild bilateral lower extremity edema. No focal neurological deficits. CHADS2-VASc score 4.

imaging_studies.ECG: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, rate 128. No acute ST changes. No prior ECG for comparison on file.
imaging_studies.Echocardiogram: Last echo 2023: LVEF 55%, mild LA enlargement, no significant valvular disease.

clinicians_initial_plan: Diagnosis: New-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Start amiodarone 200 mg three times daily for one week, then 200 mg daily for rhythm control
Continue warfarin 5 mg daily; rationale: existing anticoagulation, INR currently therapeutic
Add metoprolol tartrate 25 mg twice daily for rate control if amiodarone insufficient
intentional_pitfalls_for_demo: Amiodarone + warfarin: amiodarone potently inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, increasing warfarin AUC. INR likely to rise to 4.5+ within 5-10 days without preemptive warfarin dose reduction or replacement strategy.
Anchoring bias on rhythm control as default; rate control (metoprolol/diltiazem alone) is reasonable first-line per AHA/ACC 2023 guideline in stable patients.
CKD stage 3 affects digoxin and DOAC dosing if alternatives are considered.
